revision.text:
{{Short description|Former ruling palace of the Emperor of Japan}} [[File:Imperial Palace - panoramio (1).jpg|thumb|View through the Jomeimon gate on the Shishinden main hall]]
The {{nihongo|'''Kyōto Imperial Palace'''|京都御所|Kyōto-gosho}} is the former [[:en:Palace|Palace]] of the [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperor of Japan]], located in [[:en:Kamigyō-ku, Kyoto|Kamigyō-ku]], [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. Since the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]] in 1869, the Emperors have resided at the [[Tokyo Imperial Palace]], while the preservation of the Kyoto Imperial Palace was ordered in 1877.<ref>{{cite web |url=http://www.meijijingu.or.jp/about/3-3.html |title=明治神宮-明治神宮とは- |website=www.meijijingu.or.jp |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20070217214746/http://www.meijijingu.or.jp/about/3-3.html |archive-date=2007-02-17}} </ref> Today, the grounds are open to the public, and the [[:en:Imperial Household Agency|Imperial Household Agency]] hosts public tours of the buildings several times a day.
The Kyoto Imperial Palace is the latest of the imperial palaces built in the northeastern part of the old capital of [[:en:Heian-kyō|Heian-kyō]] (now known as [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]]) after the abandonment of the [[:en:Heian Palace|Heian Palace]] that was located to the west of the current palace during the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]]. The Palace lost much of its function at the time of the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]], when the capital functions were moved to [[Tokyo]] in 1869. However, [[:en:Emperor Taishō|Emperor Taishō]]<ref name="Saito">{{cite web |last1=Saitō |first1=Katsuhisa |title=Japan's First Modern Enthronement: The Ceremonies for Emperor Taishō in 1915 |url=https://www.nippon.com/en/japan-topics/c06113/japan%E2%80%99s-first-modern-enthronement-the-ceremonies-for-emperor-taisho-in-1915.html |website=Nippon.com |access-date=7 March 2022 |date=8 November 2019}}</ref> and [[:en:Hirohito|Shōwa]]<ref name="Kitano">{{cite web |last1=Kitano |first1=Ryuichi |title=Rare footage shows Hirohito's enthronement rituals in Kyoto |url=https://www.asahi.com/ajw/articles/13057684#:~:text=It%20depicts%20various%20events%20held,in%20Kyoto%2C%20the%20ancient%20capital. |website=The Asahi Shimbun |access-date=7 March 2022 |date=12 November 2019}}</ref> still had their [[Enthronement of the Japanese emperor|enthronement ceremonies]] at the palace.
== Layout == [[File:Kyoto Gyoen National Garden aerial photograph 2020.jpg|thumb|Aerial view of the Kyōto-gyoen in 2020 with the Imperial Palace in the northern part]]
The Palace is situated in the {{Nihongo|Kyōto-gyoen|京都御苑}}, a large rectangular enclosure {{convert|1300|m}} north to south and {{convert|700|m}} east to west. It also contains the [[Sentō Imperial Palace]] gardens and the [[:en:Kyoto State Guest House|Kyoto State Guest House]]. The estate dates from the early [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] when the residence of high court nobles were grouped close together with the palace and the area walled. When the capital was moved to Tokyo, the residences of the court nobles were demolished and most of Kyōto Gyoen is now a park open to the public.
The Imperial Palace has been officially located in this area since the final abandonment of the [[:en:Heian Palace|Heian Palace]] in late 12th century. However, it was already much earlier that the ''de facto'' residence of the Emperors was often not in the {{Nihongo|[[Heian Palace#Inner Palace|Inner Palace]]|内裏|dairi}} of the original Heian period palace, but in one of the {{Nihongo|''temporary residences''|里内裏|sato-dairi}} in this part of the city and often provided to the Emperor by powerful noble families. The present palace is a direct successor—after iterations of rebuilding—to one of these ''sato-dairi'' palaces, the {{Nihongo|Tsuchimikado Dono|土御門殿|Tsuchimikado-dono}} of the [[Fujiwara clan]]. The palace, like many of the oldest and most important buildings in Japan, was destroyed by fire and rebuilt many times over the course of its history. It has been destroyed and rebuilt eight times, six of them during the 250-year-long peace of the Edo period. The version currently standing was completed in 1855, with an attempt at reproducing the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]] architecture and style of the original ''dairi'' of the Heian Palace.
The grounds include a number of buildings, along with the imperial residence. The neighboring building to the north is the {{Nihongo|''sentō''|仙洞}}, or residence of the retired Emperor, and beyond that, across [[:en:Imadegawa Street|Imadegawa Street]], sits [[:en:Doshisha University|Doshisha University]]. The [[:en:Imperial Household Agency|Imperial Household Agency]] maintains the building and the grounds and also runs public tours.<ref>{{cite web |url=http://www.kyoto-np.co.jp/kp/koto/gosyo/gosyo_e.html |title=Visiting Procedures for Foreigners -- Kyoto Imperial Palace |access-date=2007-09-12 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20070905181258/http://www.kyoto-np.co.jp/kp/koto/gosyo/gosyo_e.html |archive-date=2007-09-05 |url-status=dead }}</ref>
== Structures == The main buildings are, among other halls, the {{Nihongo|Shishinden|紫宸殿|extra=Hall for State Ceremonies}}, {{Nihongo|Seiryōden|清涼殿|extra=Emperor's Habitual Residence}}, {{Nihongo|Kogosho|小御所|extra=Court Room}}, {{Nihongo|Ogakumonjo|御学問所|extra=Imperial Study or Library<ref>{{cite web|url=http://dictionary.goo.ne.jp/leaf/jn2/76245/m0u/|title=ごがくもんじょ【御学問所】の意味 - 国語辞書 - goo辞書|work=goo辞書}}</ref>}}, and a number of residences for the Empress, high-Ranking aristocrats and government officials.
=== Okurumayose === Dignitaries with special permission for official visits used to enter the palace through the {{Nihongo|''Okurumayose''|御車寄}} entrance.
=== Shodaibunoma === The {{Nihongo|''Shodaibunoma''|諸大夫の間}} building was used as a waiting room for dignitaries on their official visits to the palace. They were ushered into three different anterooms according to their Ranks.
=== Shinmikurumayose === The {{Nihongo|''Shinmikurumayose''|新御車寄}} structure was built as a new carriage entrance on the occasion of the enthronement ceremony of Emperor Taisho in 1915.
=== Gates === [[Image:Kyotopalace.jpg|thumb|{{Nihongo|''Kenreimon''|建礼門}}, one of the main entrance gates from the outer to the inner courtyard]]
For state ceremonies, the dignitaries would enter through the {{Nihongo|''Kenreimon''|建礼門}}, which has a cypress-wood roof, and is supported by four unpainted wooden pillars. This gate would have been used on the rare occasions of the Emperor welcoming a foreign diplomat or dignitary, as well as for many other important state ceremonies. Passing through the ''Kenreimon'', the inner gate ''Jomeimon'' would appear, which is painted in vermilion and roofed in tile. This leads to the ''Shishin-den'', which is the Hall for State Ceremonies. The ''Gekkamon'' is a smaller gate on the west side of the main courtyard.
The annual [[Aoi Matsuri]] in May is the procession of the ''Saiō-Dai'', historically a priestess of the imperial house, to the [[Shimogamo Shrine]] and [[Kamigamo Shrine]]. The procession departs in front of the ''Kenreimon''.
Another gate in the outer courtyard is the ''Kenshunmon'', which has a similar architectural style to the ''Kenreimon''. Located next to the ''Kenshunmon'' is a square where the traditional ball game ''[[:en:Kemari|Kemari]]'' is played.
<gallery> File:Imperial palace 1.JPG|''Jomeimon'' File:Kenshun-Mon.JPG|''Kenshunmon'' </gallery>
=== Shunkōden === [[File:Shunko-Den.JPG|thumb|Shunkōden]]
The {{Nihongo|''Shunkōden''|春興殿}} was constructed to house the sacred mirror on the occasion of the enthronement ceremony of Emperor Taisho in 1915. The roof is modern in that it is made out of copper and not wooden shingles.
=== Shishinden === [[File:Imperial Palace in Kyoto - looking into south gate of main building 3.JPG|thumb|View through the ''Jomeimon'' on ''Shishinden'' main hall]]
The {{Nihongo|Shishinden|紫宸殿}} is the most important ceremonial building within the palace grounds. The enthronement ceremonies of Emperor Taisho and Emperor Showa took place here. The hall is {{convert|33|by|23|m}} in size, and features a traditional architectural style, with a gabled and hipped roof. On either side of its main stairway were planted trees which would become very famous and sacred, a cherry (''sakura'') on the eastern, left side, and a ''tachibana'' orange tree on the right to the west. The garden of white gravel played an important role in the ceremony.
The center of the Shishin-den is surrounded by a {{Nihongo|''hisashi''|庇}}, a long, thin hallway which surrounded the main wing of an aristocrat's home, in traditional Heian architecture. Within this is a wide open space, crossed by boarded-over sections, leading to the central throne room.
<gallery> File:Enthronement of Emperor Taisho 1.JPG|Enthronement of [[:en:Emperor Taishō|Emperor Taishō]] in 1915 File:Sakon no Sakura.JPG|''Sakon no Sakura'' tree File:Ukon no Tachibana.JPG|''Ukon no Tachibana'' tree </gallery>
==== Takamikura ==== [[File:Imperial Throne of Shishinden in Kyoto Imperial Palace.jpg|thumb|left|A glimpse of the Imperial throne in the main hall]]
The {{Nihongo|''Takamikura''|高御座}} is the [[:en:Chrysanthemum Throne|Imperial throne]]. It has been used on the occasion of the [[Enthronement of the Japanese emperor|enthronement ceremonies]] commencing in 707 in the reign of [[:en:Empress Genmei|Empress Genmei]]. The present throne was modeled on the original design, constructed in 1913, two years before the enthronement of [[:en:Emperor Taishō|Emperor Taishō]]. The actual throne is a chair in black lacquer, placed under an octagonal canopy resting on a three-tiered dais painted with black lacquer with balustrades of vermilion. On both sides of the throne are two little tables, where two of the three [[:en:Imperial Regalia of Japan|Imperial regalia]] (the sword and the jewel), and the [[:en:Privy Seal of Japan|privy seal]] and [[State Seal of Japan|state seal]] would be placed. On top of the canopy is a statue of a large phoenix called ''[[:en:hō-ō|hō-ō]]''. Surrounding the canopy are eight small phoenixes, jewels and mirrors. Hanging from the canopy are metal ornaments and curtains.
The sliding door that hid the Emperor from view is called {{Nihongo|''kenjō no shōji''|[[:ja:賢聖障子|賢聖障子]]}}, and has an image of 32 celestial saints painted upon it, which became one of the primary models for all of Heian period painting.
==== Michodai ==== The {{Nihongo|''Michodai''|御帳台}} is the August Seat of the Empress. The current throne was constructed in 1913. Its colour and shape are the same as the ''Takamikura'', but is slightly smaller and more simple in comparison. The canopy is decorated with a statue of the mythical bird ''ranchō''.
The Imperial throne is always placed in the center of the main hall, the ''michodai'' to the right of it. Both thrones are kept away from public view through screens called ''[[sudare|misu]]''.
=== Seiryōden === [[Image:Seiryoden.jpg|thumb|''Seiryōden'']]
The {{Nihongo|''Seiryōden''|清涼殿}} sits to the west of the Shishin-den, facing east. It, too, has a hipped and gabled roof, and is primarily cypress wood. Originally a place where the Emperor would conduct his own personal affairs, the Seiryō-den was later used for various gatherings and meetings as well. In the center is an area where the Emperor would rest, and on the east side of the hall, an area of two tatami was set aside for dignitaries and aristocrats to sit. Here was where the Emperor could conduct formal affairs. On the north side of the hall was an enclosed area where the Emperor would sleep at night; later, Emperors began to use the official residence. The west side was set aside for the Emperor's breakfasts, and also contained the lavatories, while the south side was used by the keeper of the Imperial Archives. This area contained paintings by the masters of the [[Tosa school]], and just outside, various rare bamboos were planted.
The original structure was built as the Emperor's residence at the end of the 8th century and was used until the 11th century. The ''Seiryō-den'' was rebuilt in this location in 1790 CE, on a smaller scale than the original building but preserving the original structure.
=== Kogosho === The {{Nihongo|''Kogosho''|小御所}} is a place where the Emperor received bannermen under the direct control of the Tokugawa shogun (''[[:en:Buke (Japan)|buke]]''). It was also used for some rituals. This distinctive building shows a blend of architectural elements of ''shinden zukuri'' and ''shoin zukuri'' styles.
The Kogosho Conference was held here on the night of December 9, 1867, the declaration of the [[Meiji Restoration|restoration of imperial rule]] (''osei fukko''). The structure burnt down in 1954 and was reconstructed in 1958.
=== Ogakumonjo === The study hall {{Nihongo|''Ogakumonjo''|御学問所}} was for reading rites, a monthly poetry recital and also a place the Emperor received nobles. It is a ''shoin zukuri'' style building with an ''irimoya hiwadabuki'' roof.
=== Otsunegoten === The {{Nihongo|''Otsunegoten''|御常御殿}} was built and used as the Emperor's residence from 1590 until the capital was transferred to Tokyo in 1869. It is the largest structure of the palace with fifteen rooms. Facing it is the ''Gonaeitei'' garden.
=== Osuzumisho === [[File:Imperial Palace in Kyoto - gate to house for emperor's family.JPG|thumb|''Osuzumisho'']]
The {{Nihongo|''Osuzumisho''|御涼所}} is the summer residence for the Emperor.
=== Koshun === The {{Nihongo|''Koshun''|迎春}} is a study hall that was used by [[:en:Emperor Komei|Emperor Komei]], who reigned from 1846 to 1866.
=== Omima === The {{Nihongo|''Omima''|御三間}} was used for unofficial ceremonies such as the [[Star Festival]] and the [[:en:Bon festival|Bon festival]].
The Palace's {{Nihongo|''[[Suzaku-mon]]''|朱雀門}} is walking distance from [[:en:Japan Railway|JR]] [[:en:Nijō Station (Kyoto)|Nijō Station]].
<gallery> Image:Sarugatsuji of Kyoto Imperial Palace.jpg|The ''[[Sarugatsuji]]'' Image:Kyoto palace garden01.jpg|{{Nihongo|''Oike-niwa''|御池庭}} garden and pond </gallery>
==See also== * [[:en:Kyoto Gyoen National Garden|Kyoto Gyoen National Garden]]

revision.text:
{{Short description|Former ruling palace of the Emperor of Japan}} [[File:Imperial Palace - panoramio (1).jpg|thumb|View through the Jomeimon gate on the Shishinden main hall]]
The {{nihongo|'''Kyōto Imperial Palace'''|京都御所|Kyōto-gosho}} is the former [[:en:Palace|Palace]] of the [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperor of Japan]], located in [[:en:Kamigyō-ku, Kyoto|Kamigyō-ku]], [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. Since the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]] in 1869, the Emperors have resided at the [[Tokyo Imperial Palace]], while the preservation of the Kyoto Imperial Palace was ordered in 1877.<ref>{{cite web |url=http://www.meijijingu.or.jp/about/3-3.html |title=明治神宮-明治神宮とは- |website=www.meijijingu.or.jp |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20070217214746/http://www.meijijingu.or.jp/about/3-3.html |archive-date=2007-02-17}} </ref> Today, the grounds are open to the public, and the [[:en:Imperial Household Agency|Imperial Household Agency]] hosts public tours of the buildings several times a day.
The Kyoto Imperial Palace is the latest of the imperial palaces built in the northeastern part of the old capital of [[:en:Heian-kyō|Heian-kyō]] (now known as [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]]) after the abandonment of the [[:en:Heian Palace|Heian Palace]] that was located to the west of the current palace during the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]]. The Palace lost much of its function at the time of the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]], when the capital functions were moved to [[Tokyo]] in 1869. However, [[:en:Emperor Taishō|Emperor Taishō]]<ref name="Saito">{{cite web |last1=Saitō |first1=Katsuhisa |title=Japan's First Modern Enthronement: The Ceremonies for Emperor Taishō in 1915 |url=https://www.nippon.com/en/japan-topics/c06113/japan%E2%80%99s-first-modern-enthronement-the-ceremonies-for-emperor-taisho-in-1915.html |website=Nippon.com |access-date=7 March 2022 |date=8 November 2019}}</ref> and [[:en:Hirohito|Shōwa]]<ref name="Kitano">{{cite web |last1=Kitano |first1=Ryuichi |title=Rare footage shows Hirohito's enthronement rituals in Kyoto |url=https://www.asahi.com/ajw/articles/13057684#:~:text=It%20depicts%20various%20events%20held,in%20Kyoto%2C%20the%20ancient%20capital. |website=The Asahi Shimbun |access-date=7 March 2022 |date=12 November 2019}}</ref> still had their [[Enthronement of the Japanese emperor|enthronement ceremonies]] at the palace.
== Layout == [[File:Kyoto Gyoen National Garden aerial photograph 2020.jpg|thumb|Aerial view of the Kyōto-gyoen in 2020 with the Imperial Palace in the northern part]]
The Palace is situated in the {{Nihongo|Kyōto-gyoen|京都御苑}}, a large rectangular enclosure {{convert|1300|m}} north to south and {{convert|700|m}} east to west. It also contains the [[Sentō Imperial Palace]] gardens and the [[:en:Kyoto State Guest House|Kyoto State Guest House]]. The estate dates from the early [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] when the residence of high court nobles were grouped close together with the palace and the area walled. When the capital was moved to Tokyo, the residences of the court nobles were demolished and most of Kyōto Gyoen is now a park open to the public.
The Imperial Palace has been officially located in this area since the final abandonment of the [[:en:Heian Palace|Heian Palace]] in late 12th century. However, it was already much earlier that the ''de facto'' residence of the Emperors was often not in the {{Nihongo|[[Heian Palace#Inner Palace|Inner Palace]]|内裏|dairi}} of the original Heian period palace, but in one of the {{Nihongo|''temporary residences''|里内裏|sato-dairi}} in this part of the city and often provided to the Emperor by powerful noble families. The present palace is a direct successor—after iterations of rebuilding—to one of these ''sato-dairi'' palaces, the {{Nihongo|Tsuchimikado Dono|土御門殿|Tsuchimikado-dono}} of the [[Fujiwara clan]]. The palace, like many of the oldest and most important buildings in Japan, was destroyed by fire and rebuilt many times over the course of its history. It has been destroyed and rebuilt eight times, six of them during the 250-year-long peace of the Edo period. The version currently standing was completed in 1855, with an attempt at reproducing the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]] architecture and style of the original ''dairi'' of the Heian Palace.
The grounds include a number of buildings, along with the imperial residence. The neighboring building to the north is the {{Nihongo|''sentō''|仙洞}}, or residence of the retired Emperor, and beyond that, across [[:en:Imadegawa Street|Imadegawa Street]], sits [[:en:Doshisha University|Doshisha University]]. The [[:en:Imperial Household Agency|Imperial Household Agency]] maintains the building and the grounds and also runs public tours.<ref>{{cite web |url=http://www.kyoto-np.co.jp/kp/koto/gosyo/gosyo_e.html |title=Visiting Procedures for Foreigners -- Kyoto Imperial Palace |access-date=2007-09-12 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20070905181258/http://www.kyoto-np.co.jp/kp/koto/gosyo/gosyo_e.html |archive-date=2007-09-05 |url-status=dead }}</ref>
== Structures == The main buildings are, among other halls, the {{Nihongo|Shishinden|紫宸殿|extra=Hall for State Ceremonies}}, {{Nihongo|Seiryōden|清涼殿|extra=Emperor's Habitual Residence}}, {{Nihongo|Kogosho|小御所|extra=Court Room}}, {{Nihongo|Ogakumonjo|御学問所|extra=Imperial Study or Library<ref>{{cite web|url=http://dictionary.goo.ne.jp/leaf/jn2/76245/m0u/|title=ごがくもんじょ【御学問所】の意味 - 国語辞書 - goo辞書|work=goo辞書}}</ref>}}, and a number of residences for the Empress, high-Ranking aristocrats and government officials.
=== Okurumayose === Dignitaries with special permission for official visits used to enter the palace through the {{Nihongo|''Okurumayose''|御車寄}} entrance.
=== Shodaibunoma === The {{Nihongo|''Shodaibunoma''|諸大夫の間}} building was used as a waiting room for dignitaries on their official visits to the palace. They were ushered into three different anterooms according to their Ranks.
=== Shinmikurumayose === The {{Nihongo|''Shinmikurumayose''|新御車寄}} structure was built as a new carriage entrance on the occasion of the enthronement ceremony of Emperor Taisho in 1915.
=== Gates === [[Image:Kyotopalace.jpg|thumb|{{Nihongo|''Kenreimon''|建礼門}}, one of the main entrance gates from the outer to the inner courtyard]]
For state ceremonies, the dignitaries would enter through the {{Nihongo|''Kenreimon''|建礼門}}, which has a cypress-wood roof, and is supported by four unpainted wooden pillars. This gate would have been used on the rare occasions of the Emperor welcoming a foreign diplomat or dignitary, as well as for many other important state ceremonies. Passing through the ''Kenreimon'', the inner gate ''Jomeimon'' would appear, which is painted in vermilion and roofed in tile. This leads to the ''Shishin-den'', which is the Hall for State Ceremonies. The ''Gekkamon'' is a smaller gate on the west side of the main courtyard.
The annual [[Aoi Matsuri]] in May is the procession of the ''Saiō-Dai'', historically a priestess of the imperial house, to the [[Shimogamo Shrine]] and [[Kamigamo Shrine]]. The procession departs in front of the ''Kenreimon''.
Another gate in the outer courtyard is the ''Kenshunmon'', which has a similar architectural style to the ''Kenreimon''. Located next to the ''Kenshunmon'' is a square where the traditional ball game ''[[:en:Kemari|Kemari]]'' is played.
<gallery> File:Imperial palace 1.JPG|''Jomeimon'' File:Kenshun-Mon.JPG|''Kenshunmon'' </gallery>
=== Shunkōden === [[File:Shunko-Den.JPG|thumb|Shunkōden]]
The {{Nihongo|''Shunkōden''|春興殿}} was constructed to house the sacred mirror on the occasion of the enthronement ceremony of Emperor Taisho in 1915. The roof is modern in that it is made out of copper and not wooden shingles.
=== Shishinden === [[File:Imperial Palace in Kyoto - looking into south gate of main building 3.JPG|thumb|View through the ''Jomeimon'' on ''Shishinden'' main hall]]
The {{Nihongo|Shishinden|紫宸殿}} is the most important ceremonial building within the palace grounds. The enthronement ceremonies of Emperor Taisho and Emperor Showa took place here. The hall is {{convert|33|by|23|m}} in size, and features a traditional architectural style, with a gabled and hipped roof. On either side of its main stairway were planted trees which would become very famous and sacred, a cherry (''sakura'') on the eastern, left side, and a ''tachibana'' orange tree on the right to the west. The garden of white gravel played an important role in the ceremony.
The center of the Shishin-den is surrounded by a {{Nihongo|''hisashi''|庇}}, a long, thin hallway which surrounded the main wing of an aristocrat's home, in traditional Heian architecture. Within this is a wide open space, crossed by boarded-over sections, leading to the central throne room.
<gallery> File:Enthronement of Emperor Taisho 1.JPG|Enthronement of [[:en:Emperor Taishō|Emperor Taishō]] in 1915 File:Sakon no Sakura.JPG|''Sakon no Sakura'' tree File:Ukon no Tachibana.JPG|''Ukon no Tachibana'' tree </gallery>
==== Takamikura ==== [[File:Imperial Throne of Shishinden in Kyoto Imperial Palace.jpg|thumb|left|A glimpse of the Imperial throne in the main hall]]
The {{Nihongo|''Takamikura''|高御座}} is the [[:en:Chrysanthemum Throne|Imperial throne]]. It has been used on the occasion of the [[Enthronement of the Japanese emperor|enthronement ceremonies]] commencing in 707 in the reign of [[:en:Empress Genmei|Empress Genmei]]. The present throne was modeled on the original design, constructed in 1913, two years before the enthronement of [[:en:Emperor Taishō|Emperor Taishō]]. The actual throne is a chair in black lacquer, placed under an octagonal canopy resting on a three-tiered dais painted with black lacquer with balustrades of vermilion. On both sides of the throne are two little tables, where two of the three [[:en:Imperial Regalia of Japan|Imperial regalia]] (the sword and the jewel), and the [[:en:Privy Seal of Japan|privy seal]] and [[State Seal of Japan|state seal]] would be placed. On top of the canopy is a statue of a large phoenix called ''[[:en:hō-ō|hō-ō]]''. Surrounding the canopy are eight small phoenixes, jewels and mirrors. Hanging from the canopy are metal ornaments and curtains.
The sliding door that hid the Emperor from view is called {{Nihongo|''kenjō no shōji''|[[:ja:賢聖障子|賢聖障子]]}}, and has an image of 32 celestial saints painted upon it, which became one of the primary models for all of Heian period painting.
==== Michodai ==== The {{Nihongo|''Michodai''|御帳台}} is the August Seat of the Empress. The current throne was constructed in 1913. Its colour and shape are the same as the ''Takamikura'', but is slightly smaller and more simple in comparison. The canopy is decorated with a statue of the mythical bird ''ranchō''.
The Imperial throne is always placed in the center of the main hall, the ''michodai'' to the right of it. Both thrones are kept away from public view through screens called ''[[sudare|misu]]''.
=== Seiryōden === [[Image:Seiryoden.jpg|thumb|''Seiryōden'']]
The {{Nihongo|''Seiryōden''|清涼殿}} sits to the west of the Shishin-den, facing east. It, too, has a hipped and gabled roof, and is primarily cypress wood. Originally a place where the Emperor would conduct his own personal affairs, the Seiryō-den was later used for various gatherings and meetings as well. In the center is an area where the Emperor would rest, and on the east side of the hall, an area of two tatami was set aside for dignitaries and aristocrats to sit. Here was where the Emperor could conduct formal affairs. On the north side of the hall was an enclosed area where the Emperor would sleep at night; later, Emperors began to use the official residence. The west side was set aside for the Emperor's breakfasts, and also contained the lavatories, while the south side was used by the keeper of the Imperial Archives. This area contained paintings by the masters of the [[Tosa school]], and just outside, various rare bamboos were planted.
The original structure was built as the Emperor's residence at the end of the 8th century and was used until the 11th century. The ''Seiryō-den'' was rebuilt in this location in 1790 CE, on a smaller scale than the original building but preserving the original structure.
=== Kogosho === The {{Nihongo|''Kogosho''|小御所}} is a place where the Emperor received bannermen under the direct control of the Tokugawa shogun (''[[:en:Buke (Japan)|buke]]''). It was also used for some rituals. This distinctive building shows a blend of architectural elements of ''shinden zukuri'' and ''shoin zukuri'' styles.
The Kogosho Conference was held here on the night of December 9, 1867, the declaration of the [[Meiji Restoration|restoration of imperial rule]] (''osei fukko''). The structure burnt down in 1954 and was reconstructed in 1958.
=== Ogakumonjo === The study hall {{Nihongo|''Ogakumonjo''|御学問所}} was for reading rites, a monthly poetry recital and also a place the Emperor received nobles. It is a ''shoin zukuri'' style building with an ''irimoya hiwadabuki'' roof.
=== Otsunegoten === The {{Nihongo|''Otsunegoten''|御常御殿}} was built and used as the Emperor's residence from 1590 until the capital was transferred to Tokyo in 1869. It is the largest structure of the palace with fifteen rooms. Facing it is the ''Gonaeitei'' garden.
=== Osuzumisho === [[File:Imperial Palace in Kyoto - gate to house for emperor's family.JPG|thumb|''Osuzumisho'']]
The {{Nihongo|''Osuzumisho''|御涼所}} is the summer residence for the Emperor.
=== Koshun === The {{Nihongo|''Koshun''|迎春}} is a study hall that was used by [[:en:Emperor Komei|Emperor Komei]], who reigned from 1846 to 1866.
=== Omima === The {{Nihongo|''Omima''|御三間}} was used for unofficial ceremonies such as the [[Star Festival]] and the [[:en:Bon festival|Bon festival]].
The Palace's {{Nihongo|''[[Suzaku-mon]]''|朱雀門}} is walking distance from [[:en:Japan Railway|JR]] [[:en:Nijō Station (Kyoto)|Nijō Station]].
<gallery> Image:Sarugatsuji of Kyoto Imperial Palace.jpg|The ''[[:en:Sarugatsuji|Sarugatsuji]]'' Image:Kyoto palace garden01.jpg|{{Nihongo|''Oike-niwa''|御池庭}} garden and pond </gallery>
==See also== * [[:en:Kyoto Gyoen National Garden|Kyoto Gyoen National Garden]]

[[Category:Pages without translation templates]] <!--enwiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[be:Імператарскі палац у Кіёта]] [[ca:Palau Imperial de Kyoto]] [[ceb:Kyoto Imperial Palace]] [[cs:Kjótský císařský palác]] [[de:Kaiserpalast Kyōto]] [[es:Palacio Imperial de Kioto]] [[eu:Kyotoko jauregi inperiala]] [[fa:کاخ امپراتوری کیوتو]] [[fi:Kioton keisarillinen palatsi]] [[fr:Kyōto-gosho]] [[hr:Carska palača u Kyotu]] [[id:Istana Kekaisaran Kyoto]] [[it:Palazzo imperiale di Kyoto]] [[ja:京都御所]] [[ko:교토 어소]] [[la:Palatium Imperatorium Kyoto]] [[my:ကျိုတိုဘုရင့်နန်းတော်]] [[pt:Kyōto Gosho]] [[ru:Императорский дворец в Киото]] [[sl:Kjotska cesarska palača]] [[sv:Kejsarpalatset i Kyoto]] [[ta:கியோட்டோ அரண்மனை]] [[th:พระราชวังหลวงเกียวโต]] [[tr:Kyoto İmparatorluk Sarayı]] [[uk:Кіотський Імператорський палац]] [[wuu:京都御所]] [[zh:京都御所]]

revision.text:
{{Short description|Former ruling palace of the Emperor of Japan}} [[File:Imperial Palace - panoramio (1).jpg|thumb|View through the Jomeimon gate on the Shishinden main hall]]
The {{nihongo|'''Kyōto Imperial Palace'''|京都御所|Kyōto-gosho}} is the former [[:en:Palace|Palace]] of the [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperor of Japan]], located in [[:en:Kamigyō-ku, Kyoto|Kamigyō-ku]], [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. Since the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]] in 1869, the Emperors have resided at the [[Tokyo Imperial Palace]], while the preservation of the Kyoto Imperial Palace was ordered in 1877.<ref>{{cite web |url=http://www.meijijingu.or.jp/about/3-3.html |title=明治神宮-明治神宮とは- |website=www.meijijingu.or.jp |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20070217214746/http://www.meijijingu.or.jp/about/3-3.html |archive-date=2007-02-17}} </ref> Today, the grounds are open to the public, and the [[:en:Imperial Household Agency|Imperial Household Agency]] hosts public tours of the buildings several times a day.
The Kyoto Imperial Palace is the latest of the imperial palaces built in the northeastern part of the old capital of [[:en:Heian-kyō|Heian-kyō]] (now known as [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]]) after the abandonment of the [[:en:Heian Palace|Heian Palace]] that was located to the west of the current palace during the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]]. The Palace lost much of its function at the time of the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]], when the capital functions were moved to [[Tokyo]] in 1869. However, [[:en:Emperor Taishō|Emperor Taishō]]<ref name="Saito">{{cite web |last1=Saitō |first1=Katsuhisa |title=Japan's First Modern Enthronement: The Ceremonies for Emperor Taishō in 1915 |url=https://www.nippon.com/en/japan-topics/c06113/japan%E2%80%99s-first-modern-enthronement-the-ceremonies-for-emperor-taisho-in-1915.html |website=Nippon.com |access-date=7 March 2022 |date=8 November 2019}}</ref> and [[:en:Hirohito|Shōwa]]<ref name="Kitano">{{cite web |last1=Kitano |first1=Ryuichi |title=Rare footage shows Hirohito's enthronement rituals in Kyoto |url=https://www.asahi.com/ajw/articles/13057684#:~:text=It%20depicts%20various%20events%20held,in%20Kyoto%2C%20the%20ancient%20capital. |website=The Asahi Shimbun |access-date=7 March 2022 |date=12 November 2019}}</ref> still had their [[Enthronement of the Japanese emperor|enthronement ceremonies]] at the palace.
== Layout == [[File:Kyoto Gyoen National Garden aerial photograph 2020.jpg|thumb|Aerial view of the Kyōto-gyoen in 2020 with the Imperial Palace in the northern part]]
The Palace is situated in the {{Nihongo|Kyōto-gyoen|京都御苑}}, a large rectangular enclosure {{convert|1300|m}} north to south and {{convert|700|m}} east to west. It also contains the [[:en:Sentō Imperial Palace|Sentō Imperial Palace]] gardens and the [[:en:Kyoto State Guest House|Kyoto State Guest House]]. The estate dates from the early [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] when the residence of high court nobles were grouped close together with the palace and the area walled. When the capital was moved to Tokyo, the residences of the court nobles were demolished and most of Kyōto Gyoen is now a park open to the public.
The Imperial Palace has been officially located in this area since the final abandonment of the [[:en:Heian Palace|Heian Palace]] in late 12th century. However, it was already much earlier that the ''de facto'' residence of the Emperors was often not in the {{Nihongo|[[Heian Palace#Inner Palace|Inner Palace]]|内裏|dairi}} of the original Heian period palace, but in one of the {{Nihongo|''temporary residences''|里内裏|sato-dairi}} in this part of the city and often provided to the Emperor by powerful noble families. The present palace is a direct successor—after iterations of rebuilding—to one of these ''sato-dairi'' palaces, the {{Nihongo|Tsuchimikado Dono|土御門殿|Tsuchimikado-dono}} of the [[Fujiwara clan]]. The palace, like many of the oldest and most important buildings in Japan, was destroyed by fire and rebuilt many times over the course of its history. It has been destroyed and rebuilt eight times, six of them during the 250-year-long peace of the Edo period. The version currently standing was completed in 1855, with an attempt at reproducing the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]] architecture and style of the original ''dairi'' of the Heian Palace.
The grounds include a number of buildings, along with the imperial residence. The neighboring building to the north is the {{Nihongo|''sentō''|仙洞}}, or residence of the retired Emperor, and beyond that, across [[:en:Imadegawa Street|Imadegawa Street]], sits [[:en:Doshisha University|Doshisha University]]. The [[:en:Imperial Household Agency|Imperial Household Agency]] maintains the building and the grounds and also runs public tours.<ref>{{cite web |url=http://www.kyoto-np.co.jp/kp/koto/gosyo/gosyo_e.html |title=Visiting Procedures for Foreigners -- Kyoto Imperial Palace |access-date=2007-09-12 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20070905181258/http://www.kyoto-np.co.jp/kp/koto/gosyo/gosyo_e.html |archive-date=2007-09-05 |url-status=dead }}</ref>
== Structures == The main buildings are, among other halls, the {{Nihongo|Shishinden|紫宸殿|extra=Hall for State Ceremonies}}, {{Nihongo|Seiryōden|清涼殿|extra=Emperor's Habitual Residence}}, {{Nihongo|Kogosho|小御所|extra=Court Room}}, {{Nihongo|Ogakumonjo|御学問所|extra=Imperial Study or Library<ref>{{cite web|url=http://dictionary.goo.ne.jp/leaf/jn2/76245/m0u/|title=ごがくもんじょ【御学問所】の意味 - 国語辞書 - goo辞書|work=goo辞書}}</ref>}}, and a number of residences for the Empress, high-Ranking aristocrats and government officials.
=== Okurumayose === Dignitaries with special permission for official visits used to enter the palace through the {{Nihongo|''Okurumayose''|御車寄}} entrance.
=== Shodaibunoma === The {{Nihongo|''Shodaibunoma''|諸大夫の間}} building was used as a waiting room for dignitaries on their official visits to the palace. They were ushered into three different anterooms according to their Ranks.
=== Shinmikurumayose === The {{Nihongo|''Shinmikurumayose''|新御車寄}} structure was built as a new carriage entrance on the occasion of the enthronement ceremony of Emperor Taisho in 1915.
=== Gates === [[Image:Kyotopalace.jpg|thumb|{{Nihongo|''Kenreimon''|建礼門}}, one of the main entrance gates from the outer to the inner courtyard]]
For state ceremonies, the dignitaries would enter through the {{Nihongo|''Kenreimon''|建礼門}}, which has a cypress-wood roof, and is supported by four unpainted wooden pillars. This gate would have been used on the rare occasions of the Emperor welcoming a foreign diplomat or dignitary, as well as for many other important state ceremonies. Passing through the ''Kenreimon'', the inner gate ''Jomeimon'' would appear, which is painted in vermilion and roofed in tile. This leads to the ''Shishin-den'', which is the Hall for State Ceremonies. The ''Gekkamon'' is a smaller gate on the west side of the main courtyard.
The annual [[Aoi Matsuri]] in May is the procession of the ''Saiō-Dai'', historically a priestess of the imperial house, to the [[Shimogamo Shrine]] and [[Kamigamo Shrine]]. The procession departs in front of the ''Kenreimon''.
Another gate in the outer courtyard is the ''Kenshunmon'', which has a similar architectural style to the ''Kenreimon''. Located next to the ''Kenshunmon'' is a square where the traditional ball game ''[[:en:Kemari|Kemari]]'' is played.
<gallery> File:Imperial palace 1.JPG|''Jomeimon'' File:Kenshun-Mon.JPG|''Kenshunmon'' </gallery>
=== Shunkōden === [[File:Shunko-Den.JPG|thumb|Shunkōden]]
The {{Nihongo|''Shunkōden''|春興殿}} was constructed to house the sacred mirror on the occasion of the enthronement ceremony of Emperor Taisho in 1915. The roof is modern in that it is made out of copper and not wooden shingles.
=== Shishinden === [[File:Imperial Palace in Kyoto - looking into south gate of main building 3.JPG|thumb|View through the ''Jomeimon'' on ''Shishinden'' main hall]]
The {{Nihongo|Shishinden|紫宸殿}} is the most important ceremonial building within the palace grounds. The enthronement ceremonies of Emperor Taisho and Emperor Showa took place here. The hall is {{convert|33|by|23|m}} in size, and features a traditional architectural style, with a gabled and hipped roof. On either side of its main stairway were planted trees which would become very famous and sacred, a cherry (''sakura'') on the eastern, left side, and a ''tachibana'' orange tree on the right to the west. The garden of white gravel played an important role in the ceremony.
The center of the Shishin-den is surrounded by a {{Nihongo|''hisashi''|庇}}, a long, thin hallway which surrounded the main wing of an aristocrat's home, in traditional Heian architecture. Within this is a wide open space, crossed by boarded-over sections, leading to the central throne room.
<gallery> File:Enthronement of Emperor Taisho 1.JPG|Enthronement of [[:en:Emperor Taishō|Emperor Taishō]] in 1915 File:Sakon no Sakura.JPG|''Sakon no Sakura'' tree File:Ukon no Tachibana.JPG|''Ukon no Tachibana'' tree </gallery>
==== Takamikura ==== [[File:Imperial Throne of Shishinden in Kyoto Imperial Palace.jpg|thumb|left|A glimpse of the Imperial throne in the main hall]]
The {{Nihongo|''Takamikura''|高御座}} is the [[:en:Chrysanthemum Throne|Imperial throne]]. It has been used on the occasion of the [[Enthronement of the Japanese emperor|enthronement ceremonies]] commencing in 707 in the reign of [[:en:Empress Genmei|Empress Genmei]]. The present throne was modeled on the original design, constructed in 1913, two years before the enthronement of [[:en:Emperor Taishō|Emperor Taishō]]. The actual throne is a chair in black lacquer, placed under an octagonal canopy resting on a three-tiered dais painted with black lacquer with balustrades of vermilion. On both sides of the throne are two little tables, where two of the three [[:en:Imperial Regalia of Japan|Imperial regalia]] (the sword and the jewel), and the [[:en:Privy Seal of Japan|privy seal]] and [[State Seal of Japan|state seal]] would be placed. On top of the canopy is a statue of a large phoenix called ''[[:en:hō-ō|hō-ō]]''. Surrounding the canopy are eight small phoenixes, jewels and mirrors. Hanging from the canopy are metal ornaments and curtains.
The sliding door that hid the Emperor from view is called {{Nihongo|''kenjō no shōji''|[[:ja:賢聖障子|賢聖障子]]}}, and has an image of 32 celestial saints painted upon it, which became one of the primary models for all of Heian period painting.
==== Michodai ==== The {{Nihongo|''Michodai''|御帳台}} is the August Seat of the Empress. The current throne was constructed in 1913. Its colour and shape are the same as the ''Takamikura'', but is slightly smaller and more simple in comparison. The canopy is decorated with a statue of the mythical bird ''ranchō''.
The Imperial throne is always placed in the center of the main hall, the ''michodai'' to the right of it. Both thrones are kept away from public view through screens called ''[[sudare|misu]]''.
=== Seiryōden === [[Image:Seiryoden.jpg|thumb|''Seiryōden'']]
The {{Nihongo|''Seiryōden''|清涼殿}} sits to the west of the Shishin-den, facing east. It, too, has a hipped and gabled roof, and is primarily cypress wood. Originally a place where the Emperor would conduct his own personal affairs, the Seiryō-den was later used for various gatherings and meetings as well. In the center is an area where the Emperor would rest, and on the east side of the hall, an area of two tatami was set aside for dignitaries and aristocrats to sit. Here was where the Emperor could conduct formal affairs. On the north side of the hall was an enclosed area where the Emperor would sleep at night; later, Emperors began to use the official residence. The west side was set aside for the Emperor's breakfasts, and also contained the lavatories, while the south side was used by the keeper of the Imperial Archives. This area contained paintings by the masters of the [[Tosa school]], and just outside, various rare bamboos were planted.
The original structure was built as the Emperor's residence at the end of the 8th century and was used until the 11th century. The ''Seiryō-den'' was rebuilt in this location in 1790 CE, on a smaller scale than the original building but preserving the original structure.
=== Kogosho === The {{Nihongo|''Kogosho''|小御所}} is a place where the Emperor received bannermen under the direct control of the Tokugawa shogun (''[[:en:Buke (Japan)|buke]]''). It was also used for some rituals. This distinctive building shows a blend of architectural elements of ''shinden zukuri'' and ''shoin zukuri'' styles.
The Kogosho Conference was held here on the night of December 9, 1867, the declaration of the [[Meiji Restoration|restoration of imperial rule]] (''osei fukko''). The structure burnt down in 1954 and was reconstructed in 1958.
=== Ogakumonjo === The study hall {{Nihongo|''Ogakumonjo''|御学問所}} was for reading rites, a monthly poetry recital and also a place the Emperor received nobles. It is a ''shoin zukuri'' style building with an ''irimoya hiwadabuki'' roof.
=== Otsunegoten === The {{Nihongo|''Otsunegoten''|御常御殿}} was built and used as the Emperor's residence from 1590 until the capital was transferred to Tokyo in 1869. It is the largest structure of the palace with fifteen rooms. Facing it is the ''Gonaeitei'' garden.
=== Osuzumisho === [[File:Imperial Palace in Kyoto - gate to house for emperor's family.JPG|thumb|''Osuzumisho'']]
The {{Nihongo|''Osuzumisho''|御涼所}} is the summer residence for the Emperor.
=== Koshun === The {{Nihongo|''Koshun''|迎春}} is a study hall that was used by [[:en:Emperor Komei|Emperor Komei]], who reigned from 1846 to 1866.
=== Omima === The {{Nihongo|''Omima''|御三間}} was used for unofficial ceremonies such as the [[Star Festival]] and the [[:en:Bon festival|Bon festival]].
The Palace's {{Nihongo|''[[Suzaku-mon]]''|朱雀門}} is walking distance from [[:en:Japan Railway|JR]] [[:en:Nijō Station (Kyoto)|Nijō Station]].
<gallery> Image:Sarugatsuji of Kyoto Imperial Palace.jpg|The ''[[:en:Sarugatsuji|Sarugatsuji]]'' Image:Kyoto palace garden01.jpg|{{Nihongo|''Oike-niwa''|御池庭}} garden and pond </gallery>
==See also== * [[:en:Kyoto Gyoen National Garden|Kyoto Gyoen National Garden]]

revision.text:
{{Short description|Former ruling palace of the Emperor of Japan}} [[File:Imperial Palace - panoramio (1).jpg|thumb|View through the Jomeimon gate on the Shishinden main hall]]
The {{nihongo|'''Kyōto Imperial Palace'''|京都御所|Kyōto-gosho}} is the former [[:en:Palace|Palace]] of the [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperor of Japan]], located in [[:en:Kamigyō-ku, Kyoto|Kamigyō-ku]], [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. Since the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]] in 1869, the Emperors have resided at the [[Tokyo Imperial Palace]], while the preservation of the Kyoto Imperial Palace was ordered in 1877.<ref>{{cite web |url=http://www.meijijingu.or.jp/about/3-3.html |title=明治神宮-明治神宮とは- |website=www.meijijingu.or.jp |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20070217214746/http://www.meijijingu.or.jp/about/3-3.html |archive-date=2007-02-17}} </ref> Today, the grounds are open to the public, and the [[:en:Imperial Household Agency|Imperial Household Agency]] hosts public tours of the buildings several times a day.
The Kyoto Imperial Palace is the latest of the imperial palaces built in the northeastern part of the old capital of [[:en:Heian-kyō|Heian-kyō]] (now known as [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]]) after the abandonment of the [[:en:Heian Palace|Heian Palace]] that was located to the west of the current palace during the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]]. The Palace lost much of its function at the time of the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]], when the capital functions were moved to [[Tokyo]] in 1869. However, [[:en:Emperor Taishō|Emperor Taishō]]<ref name="Saito">{{cite web |last1=Saitō |first1=Katsuhisa |title=Japan's First Modern Enthronement: The Ceremonies for Emperor Taishō in 1915 |url=https://www.nippon.com/en/japan-topics/c06113/japan%E2%80%99s-first-modern-enthronement-the-ceremonies-for-emperor-taisho-in-1915.html |website=Nippon.com |access-date=7 March 2022 |date=8 November 2019}}</ref> and [[:en:Hirohito|Shōwa]]<ref name="Kitano">{{cite web |last1=Kitano |first1=Ryuichi |title=Rare footage shows Hirohito's enthronement rituals in Kyoto |url=https://www.asahi.com/ajw/articles/13057684#:~:text=It%20depicts%20various%20events%20held,in%20Kyoto%2C%20the%20ancient%20capital. |website=The Asahi Shimbun |access-date=7 March 2022 |date=12 November 2019}}</ref> still had their [[Enthronement of the Japanese emperor|enthronement ceremonies]] at the palace.
== Layout == [[File:Kyoto Gyoen National Garden aerial photograph 2020.jpg|thumb|Aerial view of the Kyōto-gyoen in 2020 with the Imperial Palace in the northern part]]
The Palace is situated in the {{Nihongo|Kyōto-gyoen|京都御苑}}, a large rectangular enclosure {{convert|1300|m}} north to south and {{convert|700|m}} east to west. It also contains the [[:en:Sentō Imperial Palace|Sentō Imperial Palace]] gardens and the [[:en:Kyoto State Guest House|Kyoto State Guest House]]. The estate dates from the early [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] when the residence of high court nobles were grouped close together with the palace and the area walled. When the capital was moved to Tokyo, the residences of the court nobles were demolished and most of Kyōto Gyoen is now a park open to the public.
The Imperial Palace has been officially located in this area since the final abandonment of the [[:en:Heian Palace|Heian Palace]] in late 12th century. However, it was already much earlier that the ''de facto'' residence of the Emperors was often not in the {{Nihongo|[[Heian Palace#Inner Palace|Inner Palace]]|内裏|dairi}} of the original Heian period palace, but in one of the {{Nihongo|''temporary residences''|里内裏|sato-dairi}} in this part of the city and often provided to the Emperor by powerful noble families. The present palace is a direct successor—after iterations of rebuilding—to one of these ''sato-dairi'' palaces, the {{Nihongo|Tsuchimikado Dono|土御門殿|Tsuchimikado-dono}} of the [[Fujiwara clan]]. The palace, like many of the oldest and most important buildings in Japan, was destroyed by fire and rebuilt many times over the course of its history. It has been destroyed and rebuilt eight times, six of them during the 250-year-long peace of the Edo period. The version currently standing was completed in 1855, with an attempt at reproducing the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]] architecture and style of the original ''dairi'' of the Heian Palace.
The grounds include a number of buildings, along with the imperial residence. The neighboring building to the north is the {{Nihongo|''sentō''|仙洞}}, or residence of the retired Emperor, and beyond that, across [[:en:Imadegawa Street|Imadegawa Street]], sits [[:en:Doshisha University|Doshisha University]]. The [[:en:Imperial Household Agency|Imperial Household Agency]] maintains the building and the grounds and also runs public tours.<ref>{{cite web |url=http://www.kyoto-np.co.jp/kp/koto/gosyo/gosyo_e.html |title=Visiting Procedures for Foreigners -- Kyoto Imperial Palace |access-date=2007-09-12 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20070905181258/http://www.kyoto-np.co.jp/kp/koto/gosyo/gosyo_e.html |archive-date=2007-09-05 |url-status=dead }}</ref>
== Structures == The main buildings are, among other halls, the {{Nihongo|Shishinden|紫宸殿|extra=Hall for State Ceremonies}}, {{Nihongo|Seiryōden|清涼殿|extra=Emperor's Habitual Residence}}, {{Nihongo|Kogosho|小御所|extra=Court Room}}, {{Nihongo|Ogakumonjo|御学問所|extra=Imperial Study or Library<ref>{{cite web|url=http://dictionary.goo.ne.jp/leaf/jn2/76245/m0u/|title=ごがくもんじょ【御学問所】の意味 - 国語辞書 - goo辞書|work=goo辞書}}</ref>}}, and a number of residences for the Empress, high-Ranking aristocrats and government officials.
=== Okurumayose === Dignitaries with special permission for official visits used to enter the palace through the {{Nihongo|''Okurumayose''|御車寄}} entrance.
=== Shodaibunoma === The {{Nihongo|''Shodaibunoma''|諸大夫の間}} building was used as a waiting room for dignitaries on their official visits to the palace. They were ushered into three different anterooms according to their Ranks.
=== Shinmikurumayose === The {{Nihongo|''Shinmikurumayose''|新御車寄}} structure was built as a new carriage entrance on the occasion of the enthronement ceremony of Emperor Taisho in 1915.
=== Gates === [[Image:Kyotopalace.jpg|thumb|{{Nihongo|''Kenreimon''|建礼門}}, one of the main entrance gates from the outer to the inner courtyard]]
For state ceremonies, the dignitaries would enter through the {{Nihongo|''Kenreimon''|建礼門}}, which has a cypress-wood roof, and is supported by four unpainted wooden pillars. This gate would have been used on the rare occasions of the Emperor welcoming a foreign diplomat or dignitary, as well as for many other important state ceremonies. Passing through the ''Kenreimon'', the inner gate ''Jomeimon'' would appear, which is painted in vermilion and roofed in tile. This leads to the ''Shishin-den'', which is the Hall for State Ceremonies. The ''Gekkamon'' is a smaller gate on the west side of the main courtyard.
The annual [[Aoi Matsuri]] in May is the procession of the ''Saiō-Dai'', historically a priestess of the imperial house, to the [[Shimogamo Shrine]] and [[Kamigamo Shrine]]. The procession departs in front of the ''Kenreimon''.
Another gate in the outer courtyard is the ''Kenshunmon'', which has a similar architectural style to the ''Kenreimon''. Located next to the ''Kenshunmon'' is a square where the traditional ball game ''[[:en:Kemari|Kemari]]'' is played.
<gallery> File:Imperial palace 1.JPG|''Jomeimon'' File:Kenshun-Mon.JPG|''Kenshunmon'' </gallery>
=== Shunkōden === [[File:Shunko-Den.JPG|thumb|Shunkōden]]
The {{Nihongo|''Shunkōden''|春興殿}} was constructed to house the sacred mirror on the occasion of the enthronement ceremony of Emperor Taisho in 1915. The roof is modern in that it is made out of copper and not wooden shingles.
=== Shishinden === [[File:Imperial Palace in Kyoto - looking into south gate of main building 3.JPG|thumb|View through the ''Jomeimon'' on ''Shishinden'' main hall]]
The {{Nihongo|Shishinden|紫宸殿}} is the most important ceremonial building within the palace grounds. The enthronement ceremonies of Emperor Taisho and Emperor Showa took place here. The hall is {{convert|33|by|23|m}} in size, and features a traditional architectural style, with a gabled and hipped roof. On either side of its main stairway were planted trees which would become very famous and sacred, a cherry (''sakura'') on the eastern, left side, and a ''tachibana'' orange tree on the right to the west. The garden of white gravel played an important role in the ceremony.
The center of the Shishin-den is surrounded by a {{Nihongo|''hisashi''|庇}}, a long, thin hallway which surrounded the main wing of an aristocrat's home, in traditional Heian architecture. Within this is a wide open space, crossed by boarded-over sections, leading to the central throne room.
<gallery> File:Enthronement of Emperor Taisho 1.JPG|Enthronement of [[:en:Emperor Taishō|Emperor Taishō]] in 1915 File:Sakon no Sakura.JPG|''Sakon no Sakura'' tree File:Ukon no Tachibana.JPG|''Ukon no Tachibana'' tree </gallery>
==== Takamikura ==== [[File:Imperial Throne of Shishinden in Kyoto Imperial Palace.jpg|thumb|left|A glimpse of the Imperial throne in the main hall]]
The {{Nihongo|''Takamikura''|高御座}} is the [[:en:Chrysanthemum Throne|Imperial throne]]. It has been used on the occasion of the [[Enthronement of the Japanese emperor|enthronement ceremonies]] commencing in 707 in the reign of [[:en:Empress Genmei|Empress Genmei]]. The present throne was modeled on the original design, constructed in 1913, two years before the enthronement of [[:en:Emperor Taishō|Emperor Taishō]]. The actual throne is a chair in black lacquer, placed under an octagonal canopy resting on a three-tiered dais painted with black lacquer with balustrades of vermilion. On both sides of the throne are two little tables, where two of the three [[:en:Imperial Regalia of Japan|Imperial regalia]] (the sword and the jewel), and the [[:en:Privy Seal of Japan|privy seal]] and [[:en:State Seal of Japan|state seal]] would be placed. On top of the canopy is a statue of a large phoenix called ''[[:en:hō-ō|hō-ō]]''. Surrounding the canopy are eight small phoenixes, jewels and mirrors. Hanging from the canopy are metal ornaments and curtains.
The sliding door that hid the Emperor from view is called {{Nihongo|''kenjō no shōji''|[[:ja:賢聖障子|賢聖障子]]}}, and has an image of 32 celestial saints painted upon it, which became one of the primary models for all of Heian period painting.
==== Michodai ==== The {{Nihongo|''Michodai''|御帳台}} is the August Seat of the Empress. The current throne was constructed in 1913. Its colour and shape are the same as the ''Takamikura'', but is slightly smaller and more simple in comparison. The canopy is decorated with a statue of the mythical bird ''ranchō''.
The Imperial throne is always placed in the center of the main hall, the ''michodai'' to the right of it. Both thrones are kept away from public view through screens called ''[[sudare|misu]]''.
=== Seiryōden === [[Image:Seiryoden.jpg|thumb|''Seiryōden'']]
The {{Nihongo|''Seiryōden''|清涼殿}} sits to the west of the Shishin-den, facing east. It, too, has a hipped and gabled roof, and is primarily cypress wood. Originally a place where the Emperor would conduct his own personal affairs, the Seiryō-den was later used for various gatherings and meetings as well. In the center is an area where the Emperor would rest, and on the east side of the hall, an area of two tatami was set aside for dignitaries and aristocrats to sit. Here was where the Emperor could conduct formal affairs. On the north side of the hall was an enclosed area where the Emperor would sleep at night; later, Emperors began to use the official residence. The west side was set aside for the Emperor's breakfasts, and also contained the lavatories, while the south side was used by the keeper of the Imperial Archives. This area contained paintings by the masters of the [[Tosa school]], and just outside, various rare bamboos were planted.
The original structure was built as the Emperor's residence at the end of the 8th century and was used until the 11th century. The ''Seiryō-den'' was rebuilt in this location in 1790 CE, on a smaller scale than the original building but preserving the original structure.
=== Kogosho === The {{Nihongo|''Kogosho''|小御所}} is a place where the Emperor received bannermen under the direct control of the Tokugawa shogun (''[[:en:Buke (Japan)|buke]]''). It was also used for some rituals. This distinctive building shows a blend of architectural elements of ''shinden zukuri'' and ''shoin zukuri'' styles.
The Kogosho Conference was held here on the night of December 9, 1867, the declaration of the [[Meiji Restoration|restoration of imperial rule]] (''osei fukko''). The structure burnt down in 1954 and was reconstructed in 1958.
=== Ogakumonjo === The study hall {{Nihongo|''Ogakumonjo''|御学問所}} was for reading rites, a monthly poetry recital and also a place the Emperor received nobles. It is a ''shoin zukuri'' style building with an ''irimoya hiwadabuki'' roof.
=== Otsunegoten === The {{Nihongo|''Otsunegoten''|御常御殿}} was built and used as the Emperor's residence from 1590 until the capital was transferred to Tokyo in 1869. It is the largest structure of the palace with fifteen rooms. Facing it is the ''Gonaeitei'' garden.
=== Osuzumisho === [[File:Imperial Palace in Kyoto - gate to house for emperor's family.JPG|thumb|''Osuzumisho'']]
The {{Nihongo|''Osuzumisho''|御涼所}} is the summer residence for the Emperor.
=== Koshun === The {{Nihongo|''Koshun''|迎春}} is a study hall that was used by [[:en:Emperor Komei|Emperor Komei]], who reigned from 1846 to 1866.
=== Omima === The {{Nihongo|''Omima''|御三間}} was used for unofficial ceremonies such as the [[Star Festival]] and the [[:en:Bon festival|Bon festival]].
The Palace's {{Nihongo|''[[Suzaku-mon]]''|朱雀門}} is walking distance from [[:en:Japan Railway|JR]] [[:en:Nijō Station (Kyoto)|Nijō Station]].
<gallery> Image:Sarugatsuji of Kyoto Imperial Palace.jpg|The ''[[:en:Sarugatsuji|Sarugatsuji]]'' Image:Kyoto palace garden01.jpg|{{Nihongo|''Oike-niwa''|御池庭}} garden and pond </gallery>
==See also== * [[:en:Kyoto Gyoen National Garden|Kyoto Gyoen National Garden]]

revision.text:
{{Short description|Former ruling palace of the Emperor of Japan}} [[File:Imperial Palace - panoramio (1).jpg|thumb|View through the Jomeimon gate on the Shishinden main hall]]
The {{nihongo|'''Kyōto Imperial Palace'''|京都御所|Kyōto-gosho}} is the former [[:en:Palace|Palace]] of the [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperor of Japan]], located in [[:en:Kamigyō-ku, Kyoto|Kamigyō-ku]], [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. Since the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]] in 1869, the Emperors have resided at the [[Tokyo Imperial Palace]], while the preservation of the Kyoto Imperial Palace was ordered in 1877.<ref>{{cite web |url=http://www.meijijingu.or.jp/about/3-3.html |title=明治神宮-明治神宮とは- |website=www.meijijingu.or.jp |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20070217214746/http://www.meijijingu.or.jp/about/3-3.html |archive-date=2007-02-17}} </ref> Today, the grounds are open to the public, and the [[:en:Imperial Household Agency|Imperial Household Agency]] hosts public tours of the buildings several times a day.
The Kyoto Imperial Palace is the latest of the imperial palaces built in the northeastern part of the old capital of [[:en:Heian-kyō|Heian-kyō]] (now known as [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]]) after the abandonment of the [[:en:Heian Palace|Heian Palace]] that was located to the west of the current palace during the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]]. The Palace lost much of its function at the time of the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]], when the capital functions were moved to [[Tokyo]] in 1869. However, [[:en:Emperor Taishō|Emperor Taishō]]<ref name="Saito">{{cite web |last1=Saitō |first1=Katsuhisa |title=Japan's First Modern Enthronement: The Ceremonies for Emperor Taishō in 1915 |url=https://www.nippon.com/en/japan-topics/c06113/japan%E2%80%99s-first-modern-enthronement-the-ceremonies-for-emperor-taisho-in-1915.html |website=Nippon.com |access-date=7 March 2022 |date=8 November 2019}}</ref> and [[:en:Hirohito|Shōwa]]<ref name="Kitano">{{cite web |last1=Kitano |first1=Ryuichi |title=Rare footage shows Hirohito's enthronement rituals in Kyoto |url=https://www.asahi.com/ajw/articles/13057684#:~:text=It%20depicts%20various%20events%20held,in%20Kyoto%2C%20the%20ancient%20capital. |website=The Asahi Shimbun |access-date=7 March 2022 |date=12 November 2019}}</ref> still had their [[Enthronement of the Japanese emperor|enthronement ceremonies]] at the palace.
== Layout == [[File:Kyoto Gyoen National Garden aerial photograph 2020.jpg|thumb|Aerial view of the Kyōto-gyoen in 2020 with the Imperial Palace in the northern part]]
The Palace is situated in the {{Nihongo|Kyōto-gyoen|京都御苑}}, a large rectangular enclosure {{convert|1300|m}} north to south and {{convert|700|m}} east to west. It also contains the [[:en:Sentō Imperial Palace|Sentō Imperial Palace]] gardens and the [[:en:Kyoto State Guest House|Kyoto State Guest House]]. The estate dates from the early [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] when the residence of high court nobles were grouped close together with the palace and the area walled. When the capital was moved to Tokyo, the residences of the court nobles were demolished and most of Kyōto Gyoen is now a park open to the public.
The Imperial Palace has been officially located in this area since the final abandonment of the [[:en:Heian Palace|Heian Palace]] in late 12th century. However, it was already much earlier that the ''de facto'' residence of the Emperors was often not in the {{Nihongo|[[Heian Palace#Inner Palace|Inner Palace]]|内裏|dairi}} of the original Heian period palace, but in one of the {{Nihongo|''temporary residences''|里内裏|sato-dairi}} in this part of the city and often provided to the Emperor by powerful noble families. The present palace is a direct successor—after iterations of rebuilding—to one of these ''sato-dairi'' palaces, the {{Nihongo|Tsuchimikado Dono|土御門殿|Tsuchimikado-dono}} of the [[Fujiwara clan]]. The palace, like many of the oldest and most important buildings in Japan, was destroyed by fire and rebuilt many times over the course of its history. It has been destroyed and rebuilt eight times, six of them during the 250-year-long peace of the Edo period. The version currently standing was completed in 1855, with an attempt at reproducing the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]] architecture and style of the original ''dairi'' of the Heian Palace.
The grounds include a number of buildings, along with the imperial residence. The neighboring building to the north is the {{Nihongo|''sentō''|仙洞}}, or residence of the retired Emperor, and beyond that, across [[:en:Imadegawa Street|Imadegawa Street]], sits [[:en:Doshisha University|Doshisha University]]. The [[:en:Imperial Household Agency|Imperial Household Agency]] maintains the building and the grounds and also runs public tours.<ref>{{cite web |url=http://www.kyoto-np.co.jp/kp/koto/gosyo/gosyo_e.html |title=Visiting Procedures for Foreigners -- Kyoto Imperial Palace |access-date=2007-09-12 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20070905181258/http://www.kyoto-np.co.jp/kp/koto/gosyo/gosyo_e.html |archive-date=2007-09-05 |url-status=dead }}</ref>
== Structures == The main buildings are, among other halls, the {{Nihongo|Shishinden|紫宸殿|extra=Hall for State Ceremonies}}, {{Nihongo|Seiryōden|清涼殿|extra=Emperor's Habitual Residence}}, {{Nihongo|Kogosho|小御所|extra=Court Room}}, {{Nihongo|Ogakumonjo|御学問所|extra=Imperial Study or Library<ref>{{cite web|url=http://dictionary.goo.ne.jp/leaf/jn2/76245/m0u/|title=ごがくもんじょ【御学問所】の意味 - 国語辞書 - goo辞書|work=goo辞書}}</ref>}}, and a number of residences for the Empress, high-Ranking aristocrats and government officials.
=== Okurumayose === Dignitaries with special permission for official visits used to enter the palace through the {{Nihongo|''Okurumayose''|御車寄}} entrance.
=== Shodaibunoma === The {{Nihongo|''Shodaibunoma''|諸大夫の間}} building was used as a waiting room for dignitaries on their official visits to the palace. They were ushered into three different anterooms according to their Ranks.
=== Shinmikurumayose === The {{Nihongo|''Shinmikurumayose''|新御車寄}} structure was built as a new carriage entrance on the occasion of the enthronement ceremony of Emperor Taisho in 1915.
=== Gates === [[Image:Kyotopalace.jpg|thumb|{{Nihongo|''Kenreimon''|建礼門}}, one of the main entrance gates from the outer to the inner courtyard]]
For state ceremonies, the dignitaries would enter through the {{Nihongo|''Kenreimon''|建礼門}}, which has a cypress-wood roof, and is supported by four unpainted wooden pillars. This gate would have been used on the rare occasions of the Emperor welcoming a foreign diplomat or dignitary, as well as for many other important state ceremonies. Passing through the ''Kenreimon'', the inner gate ''Jomeimon'' would appear, which is painted in vermilion and roofed in tile. This leads to the ''Shishin-den'', which is the Hall for State Ceremonies. The ''Gekkamon'' is a smaller gate on the west side of the main courtyard.
The annual [[Aoi Matsuri]] in May is the procession of the ''Saiō-Dai'', historically a priestess of the imperial house, to the [[Shimogamo Shrine]] and [[Kamigamo Shrine]]. The procession departs in front of the ''Kenreimon''.
Another gate in the outer courtyard is the ''Kenshunmon'', which has a similar architectural style to the ''Kenreimon''. Located next to the ''Kenshunmon'' is a square where the traditional ball game ''[[:en:Kemari|Kemari]]'' is played.
<gallery> File:Imperial palace 1.JPG|''Jomeimon'' File:Kenshun-Mon.JPG|''Kenshunmon'' </gallery>
=== Shunkōden === [[File:Shunko-Den.JPG|thumb|Shunkōden]]
The {{Nihongo|''Shunkōden''|春興殿}} was constructed to house the sacred mirror on the occasion of the enthronement ceremony of Emperor Taisho in 1915. The roof is modern in that it is made out of copper and not wooden shingles.
=== Shishinden === [[File:Imperial Palace in Kyoto - looking into south gate of main building 3.JPG|thumb|View through the ''Jomeimon'' on ''Shishinden'' main hall]]
The {{Nihongo|Shishinden|紫宸殿}} is the most important ceremonial building within the palace grounds. The enthronement ceremonies of Emperor Taisho and Emperor Showa took place here. The hall is {{convert|33|by|23|m}} in size, and features a traditional architectural style, with a gabled and hipped roof. On either side of its main stairway were planted trees which would become very famous and sacred, a cherry (''sakura'') on the eastern, left side, and a ''tachibana'' orange tree on the right to the west. The garden of white gravel played an important role in the ceremony.
The center of the Shishin-den is surrounded by a {{Nihongo|''hisashi''|庇}}, a long, thin hallway which surrounded the main wing of an aristocrat's home, in traditional Heian architecture. Within this is a wide open space, crossed by boarded-over sections, leading to the central throne room.
<gallery> File:Enthronement of Emperor Taisho 1.JPG|Enthronement of [[:en:Emperor Taishō|Emperor Taishō]] in 1915 File:Sakon no Sakura.JPG|''Sakon no Sakura'' tree File:Ukon no Tachibana.JPG|''Ukon no Tachibana'' tree </gallery>
==== Takamikura ==== [[File:Imperial Throne of Shishinden in Kyoto Imperial Palace.jpg|thumb|left|A glimpse of the Imperial throne in the main hall]]
The {{Nihongo|''Takamikura''|高御座}} is the [[:en:Chrysanthemum Throne|Imperial throne]]. It has been used on the occasion of the [[Enthronement of the Japanese emperor|enthronement ceremonies]] commencing in 707 in the reign of [[:en:Empress Genmei|Empress Genmei]]. The present throne was modeled on the original design, constructed in 1913, two years before the enthronement of [[:en:Emperor Taishō|Emperor Taishō]]. The actual throne is a chair in black lacquer, placed under an octagonal canopy resting on a three-tiered dais painted with black lacquer with balustrades of vermilion. On both sides of the throne are two little tables, where two of the three [[:en:Imperial Regalia of Japan|Imperial regalia]] (the sword and the jewel), and the [[:en:Privy Seal of Japan|privy seal]] and [[:en:State Seal of Japan|state seal]] would be placed. On top of the canopy is a statue of a large phoenix called ''[[:en:hō-ō|hō-ō]]''. Surrounding the canopy are eight small phoenixes, jewels and mirrors. Hanging from the canopy are metal ornaments and curtains.
The sliding door that hid the Emperor from view is called {{Nihongo|''kenjō no shōji''|[[:ja:賢聖障子|賢聖障子]]}}, and has an image of 32 celestial saints painted upon it, which became one of the primary models for all of Heian period painting.
==== Michodai ==== The {{Nihongo|''Michodai''|御帳台}} is the August Seat of the Empress. The current throne was constructed in 1913. Its colour and shape are the same as the ''Takamikura'', but is slightly smaller and more simple in comparison. The canopy is decorated with a statue of the mythical bird ''ranchō''.
The Imperial throne is always placed in the center of the main hall, the ''michodai'' to the right of it. Both thrones are kept away from public view through screens called ''[[:en:Sudare|misu]]''.
=== Seiryōden === [[Image:Seiryoden.jpg|thumb|''Seiryōden'']]
The {{Nihongo|''Seiryōden''|清涼殿}} sits to the west of the Shishin-den, facing east. It, too, has a hipped and gabled roof, and is primarily cypress wood. Originally a place where the Emperor would conduct his own personal affairs, the Seiryō-den was later used for various gatherings and meetings as well. In the center is an area where the Emperor would rest, and on the east side of the hall, an area of two tatami was set aside for dignitaries and aristocrats to sit. Here was where the Emperor could conduct formal affairs. On the north side of the hall was an enclosed area where the Emperor would sleep at night; later, Emperors began to use the official residence. The west side was set aside for the Emperor's breakfasts, and also contained the lavatories, while the south side was used by the keeper of the Imperial Archives. This area contained paintings by the masters of the [[Tosa school]], and just outside, various rare bamboos were planted.
The original structure was built as the Emperor's residence at the end of the 8th century and was used until the 11th century. The ''Seiryō-den'' was rebuilt in this location in 1790 CE, on a smaller scale than the original building but preserving the original structure.
=== Kogosho === The {{Nihongo|''Kogosho''|小御所}} is a place where the Emperor received bannermen under the direct control of the Tokugawa shogun (''[[:en:Buke (Japan)|buke]]''). It was also used for some rituals. This distinctive building shows a blend of architectural elements of ''shinden zukuri'' and ''shoin zukuri'' styles.
The Kogosho Conference was held here on the night of December 9, 1867, the declaration of the [[Meiji Restoration|restoration of imperial rule]] (''osei fukko''). The structure burnt down in 1954 and was reconstructed in 1958.
=== Ogakumonjo === The study hall {{Nihongo|''Ogakumonjo''|御学問所}} was for reading rites, a monthly poetry recital and also a place the Emperor received nobles. It is a ''shoin zukuri'' style building with an ''irimoya hiwadabuki'' roof.
=== Otsunegoten === The {{Nihongo|''Otsunegoten''|御常御殿}} was built and used as the Emperor's residence from 1590 until the capital was transferred to Tokyo in 1869. It is the largest structure of the palace with fifteen rooms. Facing it is the ''Gonaeitei'' garden.
=== Osuzumisho === [[File:Imperial Palace in Kyoto - gate to house for emperor's family.JPG|thumb|''Osuzumisho'']]
The {{Nihongo|''Osuzumisho''|御涼所}} is the summer residence for the Emperor.
=== Koshun === The {{Nihongo|''Koshun''|迎春}} is a study hall that was used by [[:en:Emperor Komei|Emperor Komei]], who reigned from 1846 to 1866.
=== Omima === The {{Nihongo|''Omima''|御三間}} was used for unofficial ceremonies such as the [[Star Festival]] and the [[:en:Bon festival|Bon festival]].
The Palace's {{Nihongo|''[[Suzaku-mon]]''|朱雀門}} is walking distance from [[:en:Japan Railway|JR]] [[:en:Nijō Station (Kyoto)|Nijō Station]].
<gallery> Image:Sarugatsuji of Kyoto Imperial Palace.jpg|The ''[[:en:Sarugatsuji|Sarugatsuji]]'' Image:Kyoto palace garden01.jpg|{{Nihongo|''Oike-niwa''|御池庭}} garden and pond </gallery>
==See also== * [[:en:Kyoto Gyoen National Garden|Kyoto Gyoen National Garden]]
==References== {{Reflist}}

[[sn:Kyoto Imperial Palace]] [[en:Kyoto Imperial Palace]]
[[Category:Pages without translation templates]] <!--enwiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[be:Імператарскі палац у Кіёта]] [[ca:Palau Imperial de Kyoto]] [[ceb:Kyoto Imperial Palace]] [[cs:Kjótský císařský palác]] [[de:Kaiserpalast Kyōto]] [[es:Palacio Imperial de Kioto]] [[eu:Kyotoko jauregi inperiala]] [[fa:کاخ امپراتوری کیوتو]] [[fi:Kioton keisarillinen palatsi]] [[fr:Kyōto-gosho]] [[hr:Carska palača u Kyotu]] [[id:Istana Kekaisaran Kyoto]] [[it:Palazzo imperiale di Kyoto]] [[ja:京都御所]] [[ko:교토 어소]] [[la:Palatium Imperatorium Kyoto]] [[my:ကျိုတိုဘုရင့်နန်းတော်]] [[pt:Kyōto Gosho]] [[ru:Императорский дворец в Киото]] [[sl:Kjotska cesarska palača]] [[sv:Kejsarpalatset i Kyoto]] [[ta:கியோட்டோ அரண்மனை]] [[th:พระราชวังหลวงเกียวโต]] [[tr:Kyoto İmparatorluk Sarayı]] [[uk:Кіотський Імператорський палац]] [[wuu:京都御所]] [[zh:京都御所]]
